narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination you've poured into this journey, and it's tough to receive this news about your application being rejected. The admissions model we use looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict whether someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated an 81.8% chance that you would not pass, which is the threshold that led to this decision. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an undergraduate GPA of 2.30, an LSAT score of 29.50, coming from an upper-middle-class family, and planning to attend full-time. What stood out most to the model were your school grade deciles—particularly your third-year performance—which had the strongest pull toward predicting a bar failure, as being in the bottom group there signaled higher risk compared to most students who rank around the middle of their classes. Your first-year decile also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing that early academic struggles carried significant weight. On a brighter note, your family income level nudged the prediction slightly toward success, since students from upper-middle backgrounds tend to fare better on average. Your undergraduate GPA and LSAT score each played smaller roles pushing toward failure, as they fall below the typical 3.21 GPA and 36.70 LSAT seen in the dataset, while your full-time enrollment status offered a minor positive influence, aligning with the vast majority of successful students. Overall, your academic rankings were the biggest factors tipping the scales against you, unlike many admitted students whose stronger class positions help offset other areas. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking academic advising to improve your standing, or exploring programs with bridge courses or bar prep support tailored for diverse students like yourself. Many have turned similar predictions around with targeted effort, and we're rooting for your next steps. Keep pushing—you've got this.
